message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To explain the global between-countries variance in number of deaths per million citizens (
                    <jats:italic>nD</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    ) and case fatality rate (
                    <jats:italic>CFR</jats:italic>
                    ) due to severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Systematic analysis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Worldometer, European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control, United Nations</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The explanators of
                    <jats:italic>
                      nD
                      <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>CFR</jats:italic>
                    were mathematically hypothesised and tested on publicly-available data from 88 countries with linear regression models on May 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    2020. The derived explanators – age-adjusted infection fatality rate (
                    <jats:italic>
                      IFR
                      <jats:sub>ad</jats:sub>
                      j
                    </jats:italic>
                    ) and case detection rate (
                    <jats:italic>CDR</jats:italic>
                    ) – were estimated for each country based on a SARS-CoV-2 model of China. The accuracy and agreement of the models with observed data was assessed with
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    and Bland-Altman plots, respectively. Sensitivity analyses involved removal of outliers and testing the models at five retrospective and four prospective time points.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Globally,
                    <jats:italic>
                      IFR
                      <jats:sub>adj</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    estimates varied between countries, ranging from below 0.2% in the youngest nations, to above 1.3% in Portugal, Greece, Italy, and Japan. The median estimated global
                    <jats:italic>CDR</jats:italic>
                    of SARS-CoV-2 infections on April 16
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    2020 was 12.9%, suggesting that most of the countries have a much higher number of cases than reported.
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:p>
                    At least 93% and up to 99% of the variance in
                    <jats:italic>
                      nD
                      <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    was explained by reported prevalence expressed as cases per million citizens (
                    <jats:italic>nC</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    ),
                    <jats:italic>
                      IFR
                      <jats:sub>adj</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    , and
                    <jats:italic>
                      CDR. IFR
                      <jats:sub>ad</jats:sub>
                      j
                    </jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>CDR</jats:italic>
                    accounted for up to 97% of the variance in
                    <jats:italic>CFR</jats:italic>
                    , but this model was less reliable than the
                    <jats:italic>
                      nD
                      <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    model, being sensitive to outliers (
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    as low as 67.5%).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The current differences in SARS-CoV-2 mortality between countries are driven mainly by reported prevalence of infections, age distribution, and
                    <jats:italic>CDR</jats:italic>
                    . The
                    <jats:italic>
                      nD
                      <jats:sub>pm</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    might be a more stable estimate than
                    <jats:italic>CFR</jats:italic>
                    in comparing mortality burden between countries.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Global between-countries variance in SARS-CoV-2 mortality is driven by reported prevalence, age distribution, and case detection rate

reference.unstructured: Studdert DM , Hall MA . Disease Control, Civil Liberties, and Mass Testing – Calibrating Restrictions during the Covid-19 Pandemic. N Engl J Med. 2020 Apr;
McKee M , Stuckler D . If the world fails to protect the economy, COVID-19 will damage health not just now but also in the future. Nat Med. 2020 Apr;

United Nations, Department of Economic and Social Affairs, Population Dynamics UN. World Population Prospects 2019, Online Edition. [Internet]. 2020 [cited 2020 Apr 12]. Available from: https://population.un.org/wpp/Download/Standard/Population/
UN Data, United Nations Statistics Division UN. Per capita GDP at current prices – US dollars [Internet]. 2020 [cited 2020 May 12]. Available from: https://data.un.org/Search.aspx?q=gdp

reference.article-title: Statistical methods for assessing agreement between two methods of clinical measurement
Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting: the GATHER statement

reference.unstructured: Onder G , Rezza G , Brusaferro S . Case-Fatality Rate and Characteristics of Patients Dying in Relation to COVID-19 in Italy. JAMA. 2020 Mar;
